ley here from Engineering New Zealand,

of emergency, whether local

often cited as a rule of thumb that
for every dollar you spend, uh, in readiness, uh,
that saved, avoids losing, uh,
$4 after the event.

that the bill will not achieve all the outcomes

is meaningful and it's place based
and it's engagement and collaboration.
And, uh, another point, um, recovery.
We, we strongly believe
that recovery starts in the planning phase before any event.
It, it, it begins to work as an event unfolds.
We work through transition,
but at this point in time, we have a concern
that there are not clear gateways pathways questions
for the trans move from the transition into,
into a constant state down the line.
So we would like to see some guidance about what
that transition and gateways look like.
Is it a transfer immediately to territorial authorities,
which in probably, it probably should be,
but equally there are probably times when NZTA or
or other lead agencies might be the appropriate place.
So just having some clear questions, uh, to help
that transition
or the ability to extend that transition at times to,
to have a seamless transition.
We, we use that word a lot, uh, through the process.
And our last significant point here is around schedule three
and the infrastructure, particularly
for US Rivers infrastructure
and the like monitoring, um, ensuring that, uh,
that is brought into the conversation.
It's all about preparedness and,
and the resilience of, of our catchments and, and Canterbury
and other areas of Christ, uh, New Zealand.
Equally, uh, we have other matters,
but that sits within our, um, submission role of Iwi Maori.
Uh, we have, uh, iwi Maori sitting at our keg
and, uh, group committee
and believe they should be there as per right.
And with, with full voting, uh, capabilities.
And the last point we would just really like to touch on is
how this all fits together with the wider suite of,
of changes that are happening, uh, within central government
of how things move, uh,
conversations within this bill talking about regional
councils and responsibilities when regional councils may not
exist somewhere in the near future.
So just how that's balanced out in the bill
and opportunity without requiring significant change into
the future if we have mandated in one bill, but,
but seeing change happening in another.
And just, uh, a little bit of a conversation
around the relationship between lead agencies
and our regional controllers who takes the responsibility,
what do those transitions look like?
I will stop there. Uh, I know it's been a brief run through,
but it's very similar to the Canterbury Regional Council
submission and, uh, give opportunity for questions.
Thank you so much for that. I really appreciate that.
And we have all got a copy
of your written submission as well.
We've got a question from Mike Davidson.
Uh, thanks for your submission.
I'm just wondering, do you think the bill was strong enough
in the risk reduction, um, aspect and act?
And then with that, do you think there should be a shared
responsibility between local
and central government to fund actually the, the cost of,
um, risk reduction?
So when you look at Canterbury, like some of the rivers
and stock banks, et cetera,
I, I would suggest we do, uh, we have, um,
build back which sits within civil defense,
but can we build back better so we can replace life
with like, so I think within that context,
at times we would like to build back better given, uh,
the change in environment
of land development, a whole bunch of things.
Uh, beyond the deluge, the piece
of work done on river management, um,
I think we identified something like 180 million,
$200 million worth of, of monies that has no longer put in
by central government post the,
the change from catchment boards to regional councils, um,
and RMA reform.
So I think there is a responsibility for the greater good
for some of that work, particularly
around rivers stop banks infrastructure
to provide, uh, resilience.
James, do you have any thoughts?
Yeah, uh, Mike, I like the idea of, you know,
being a shared responsibility
and, you know, we unfortunately still do see
developments going into places that end up being flooded
or landslide or whatever it is.
And that would be great if New Zealand could move to a space
where we're not seeing that as much
and you know, um, you know, people are safer
and we're not having to respond as often as we perhaps are.
So a, a shared, a shared model, um,
uh, probably makes sense.
And I think within the spatial plans
of the reorganization, simplification
of local government would be useful if we could get
agreement across, uh, as a regional council,
we could set a set of planning rules, um,
but at times overridden by, by territorial authorities
and others by, so by standardizing some of our planning,
clearly identifying the risk, then
I think we can reduce some of those issues as well.
Thanks. Thank you. Just maybe a, a final question from me.
We had, um, Leanne Daral, um,
make a submission earlier this morning.
I'm not sure if you heard her submission,
but she had, um, quite a few interesting suggestions.
And one of them was, uh, having an automated review
of events after they happen.
And I just wanted to just, um, check with you your,
your kind of view on that suggestion.
Uh, from our perspective, I think yes, we, we, in
that unfortunate situation, have had
so many events over the last 10 or 15 years
and a significant number of reviews.
The review isn't necessarily the important thing.
It's actually giving effect to the actions that,
that fall out of the review.
And, and I can hear Leanne in my mind going, it's not done
until we've actually given effect to the actions.
So, so yes, an automatic automated review,
but really clearly if we have shared responsibility of
how we give effect to the actions that,
that fall out of that review.
Yeah, and I think Kim, you know, some, um,
shared understanding across agencies, uh, local government
and national central government as to the way we do it.
So I think in our submission,
we suggest we adopt the Australian
Lessons Management program.
Mm-hmm. We did, NEMA did start working on
that here in New Zealand, and it sort of got trumped
by all these events going on.
But a a, a program like that would help at all levels
of providing consistent feedback
and learning, um, from responses.
And I would say it would be nice
to see it extend into recovery.
Um, I don't think we've actually done a proper recovery
review for any of the big events in New Zealand.
Um, or, or if we have a, they disappear into the ether.
So, um, re reviews of recovery are just as important
as reviews of response.
That's a really, um, really important point.
And also, yeah, just acknowledging the 15 year anniversary
since, um, earthquake and,
and, um, Christchurch and the Canterbury region.
Um, and yeah, a lot that we are still learning from,
from that event too.
So, uh, I think that's all that all our time now.
Um, so thank you so much for joining us
and we really appreciate, um,
your written submission as well.
And, uh, answers to questions today. Kda, thank you
For the opportunity.
Thank you so much. Yep.
Thank you, uh, members, we will now, um, suspend the meeting
until 1:40 PM which is when we have our next, um,
submitter arranged to attend.
So see you back here at 1:40 PM Okay.
